All right, onboarding your general overview slash checklist. Okay, and so this, I provide an example of a checklist of what you need at each stage of onboarding. So starting with the day of or post giving an offer, right? Say you want to make your initial offer for a phone call, right? And then if they say yes, the following applies. Send a written official offer letter, send their four Rs and their internal communication cycle, send a form of payroll or HR, so whatever they need to provide you with information wise so they can get paid. Send an NDA and a non-compete, send them your employee handbook, as well as an I-9 information verification, okay? A company you can use for this is E-Verify. This is something I know a lot of people don't do. You can get a lot of trouble with the law if you are not doing so, right? Make sure that you do not move on past this point if the new employee has not agreed to all terms and signed everything, okay? Part two, this is basically after you receive back all the information from the new hire. You're going to create contact record or credentials in your CRM, create a username for all the other software, say it's Scipio, ClickFunnels, whatever else maybe. You want to create an employee email owned by the company, so like I showed you in G Suite, you want to set that up for them. Send them a felt card or a welcome package. So whether it be a card, a card and a t-shirt, their new training swag, whatever it may be, something to make them feel super welcome to the team, right? And they get that before their first day. A welcome email packet with onboarding expectations. So people don't like to come to work on the first day having no idea what they're going to be doing, okay? So you need to give them proper expectations. An equipment release form if needed, if they're using a company phone, company computer, company iPad, whatever it may be. And then including a point of contact for all hiring process going forward, okay? For their first day or week email. So this email is going to include their day one agenda, as well as outlining their week one, an employee email with work chart and the company directory, their intake form. So maybe just five questions of getting to know them that you can share with the team. That's what an intake form would be. All of their system credentials and logins with a brief explanation of each, like, hey, what do we use ClickFunnels for? Hey, what does Jim launch for? Like all that. Scheduling week one check-ins and shadows and then adding to their calendar. So making sure their calendar is pre-populated with any meetings, shadow schedules, except that they may have. Adding them as a Facebook or Instagram friend, right? And then send their weekly reporting template and instructions to them along with their communication cycle and communication expectations, right? So you wanna make sure that this is all in one email that you can get sent to them and they can have that probably two to three days before their first day starts. Now for their first day or week, what needs to be on their agenda? Okay, so you just wanna get context right now. How do you have context? One, you wanna introduce them to the whole team. You wanna do this via email. You wanna announce it on the huddle. And you wanna announce it on Facebook, okay? So make it like a celebration. Like it's a big thing. Like you probably have a smaller team, right? Below 20 people. Like announcing a new hire is such a huge thing. You need to celebrate it, right? Second, you wanna have two times a day daily touch bases. So if they're not with you all day, make sure you touch in the morning and you touch in the end of the day just for 15 minutes just to touch base, see how they're feeling, answer any questions they have, right? Third, introduce them to all clients. You wanna do this on Facebook. You also wanna do it in person, okay? This is so important. It's gonna make them feel so important as well that you're introducing them to so many people. Peer introduction and shadowing. So meaning you introduce them to all their peers. It's the trainer to all the other trainers. And maybe they start shadowing from day two or three on. You want them to attend all of your core classes, okay? So they should be watching, learning, observing. Just doing that for at least the first week. They should study all client-facing material. So your internal plays. Your internal portals, memberships, all that. They should be learning all that inside and out. And then lastly, after week one completes, then you wanna start role-specific training. So you wanna get context before you go into their role because they don't understand how their role works within the company unless they have context, right? And they won't know what they need to know unless they understand how the company works. So that being said, that is what the general onboarding looks like.
